agent1_snippets.title: Lung Biopsy Techniques and Clinical Significance -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: New lesions on chest imaging should be evaluated by a multidisciplinary team involving chest specialists, surgeons, and radiologists to decide the next best management. [3] A lung biopsy is required when there is a need to provide a specific diagnosis. Indications for PTLB according to the BTS guidelines (in near-verbatim language) are as follows: [3] New or enlarging mass or solitary nodule on chest radiography that is not amenable to diagnosis by CT or bronchoscopy shows it is unlikely to be accessible by bronchoscopy. Multiple nodules in a patient with no previous history of malignancy, who have had a prolonged remission or more than one primary malignancy. Persistent focal infiltrates for which no diagnosis has been made by sputum, or bronchoscopy, blood culture, serology. Hilar mass.

agent1_snippets.title: Lung Biopsy Techniques and Clinical Significance -- Contraindications
agent1_snippets.content: Failure to obtain informed consent is a contraindication for the procedure according to the guidelines, and an alternative diagnostic modality must be considered. The previous history of pneumonectomy is a contraindication. However, if the lesion to be biopsied is pleural based, then the procedure can be attempted as long as the lung tissue is not traversed. If a vascular lesion or AVM is suspected on CT, the procedure should not be considered, and a multidisciplinary team meeting should be held to decide the next step in management. For patients on mechanical ventilation, lung biopsy may not be feasible due to limited access. Despite pre-procedure counseling, some patients may be uncooperative and move during the procedure. Hence, another diagnostic modality must be considered in these cases. Abnormal lung function or coagulation indices during the pre-procedure evaluation.

agent1_snippets.title: Lung Biopsy Techniques and Clinical Significance -- Clinical Significance
agent1_snippets.content: Percutaneous CT guided biopsy is a safe and efficacious method for the diagnosis of intermediate pulmonary lesions. It is indicated for lesions that appear malignant and in cases with high suspicion for malignancy. An initial CT scan helps in determining the accessibility of the lesion and the risk of pneumothorax. Results of previous studies for CT guided lung biopsy of small lesions had safe and accurate outcomes but less accurate when compared to large lesions. [15] When compared with FNA, core biopsy has been shown to have a slightly higher overall sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy with an improvement in the definitive diagnosis of benign lesions. However,  core biopsy is not recommended for lesions <10 mm due to the increased complication rates and decreased diagnostic accuracy. [13]

agent1_snippets.title: Percutaneous Lung Lesion Biopsy -- Technique or Treatment -- Post-Biopsy Management and Discharge
agent1_snippets.content: The decision to discharge a patient from the hospital, rather than admitting them for observation or treatment, is based on several factors, including the patient's condition, ability to be observed at home, and home location. Patients with a pneumothorax should be admitted or observed unless pneumothorax stability despite light activity is demonstrated, in which case the patient is only discharged if the following are present: The patient's prebiopsy testing parameters indicate that they are likely to survive subsequent pneumothorax growth while awaiting emergency responders to provide chest decompression. A responsible individual is willing to stay the night with the patient. The patient lives within 20 minutes of the medical facility or for ambulance arrival after a 911 call. Delayed pneumothorax necessitating chest tube insertion has occurred as late as 2 weeks after PLLB. [28]

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 874
agent2_snippets.content: Bronchoscopy with transbronchial lung biopsy is classified as a specific type of diagnostic bronchoscopy and biopsy of bronchus. This diagnostic bronchoscopy and biopsy of bronchus, in turn, is one of the procedures encompassed by the broader category Diagnostic procedures on lung and bronchus, which itself is a subcategory of other diagnostic procedures on lung and bronchus. The Tracheobronchial structure is identified as the direct anatomic site of the diagnostic bronchoscopy and biopsy of bronchus, and it is continuous with the organ referred to as plíce (the lungs). Accordingly, Bronchoscopy with transbronchial lung biopsy also has plíce listed as its procedure site, indicating that the lungs are directly involved in the intervention. The term bronchy is related to Diagnostic procedures on lung and bronchus and is linked to plíce, suggesting that bronchy represents a bronchoscopy‑related concept that pertains to lung anatomy. In imaging, computed tomography of chest: miliary pulmonary infiltrate records its findings at the site of plíce, reinforcing the clinical importance of lung assessment in both diagnostic and therapeutic contexts. Together, these relationships delineate a hierarchical and anatomical framework in which bronchoscopy‑based lung biopsies are performed on the tracheobronchial tree and lungs, are categorized within comprehensive lung and bronchus diagnostic procedures, and are complemented by chest CT imaging that evaluates pulmonary pathology.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 447
agent2_snippets.content: The mediastinum is a central thoracic subdivision that serves as the anatomic site for a spectrum of lower‑respiratory conditions classified under “other lower respiratory disease” (اضطراب منصفي), including mediastinal masses (كتلة منصفية), lung‑mediastinal abscesses, and mediastinal effusion. Computed tomography of the chest (ct scan chest) is the primary imaging modality for evaluating mediastinal pathology. Chest CT—performed as a diagnostic thoracic CT protocol that may begin without contrast and continue with contrast, or as CT angiography of the thorax with contrast—provides the “has_finding_method” link to a variety of mediastinal findings: mass of the posterior mediastinum, mass of the superior mediastinum, a general mediastinal mass, mediastinal mass containing calcium, and mediastinal effusion. Chest CT also identifies mediastinal‑pleural fistulas, recorded with the “has_direct_procedure_site” relationship to the mediastinum, and specifies the laterality of the fistula (bilateral, right‑sided, left‑sided). Therapeutically, closed drainage of the chest is associated with the thoracic subdivision and may be employed to manage complications such as mediastinal‑pleural fistulas. Together, the anatomical context of the mediastinum, its disease categorization, the detailed CT imaging techniques, the specific radiologic findings, and the related interventional procedures constitute an integrated framework for diagnosing and treating mediastinal and thoracic disorders.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1725
agent2_snippets.content: Inspection (procedure) is a diagnostic category that incorporates several specific methods for visual and functional assessment of internal organs. Among its techniques are mediastinoscopy and thoracoscopy performed with ventilation of the lung, which allow direct visualization and sampling of mediastinal and pleural structures while maintaining adequate respiratory support. Another method included under inspection is the combination of paracentesis and endoscopic examination of the ileum, enabling fluid analysis together with direct endoscopic evaluation of the distal small intestine. In addition, inspection procedures serve as the method of choice for other bowel diagnostic procedures, encompassing a range of endoscopic and minimally invasive approaches used to assess gastrointestinal pathology. Together, these methods provide clinicians with essential information for accurate diagnosis, treatment planning, and optimal patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1615
agent2_snippets.content: CT angiography of the thorax with contrast is a specific type of chest CT scan, representing a contrast‑enhanced angiographic protocol used to evaluate thoracic vascular structures. A diagnostic thoracic computed tomography performed initially without contrast material and subsequently with contrast material, followed by additional sections, constitutes a narrower, more detailed chest CT protocol that builds on the basic chest CT examination. Chest CT scanning is also employed as a finding method for identifying mediastinal masses, as illustrated by the use of computed tomography of the chest to detect such lesions. Together, these concepts highlight that chest CT encompasses a range of techniques—from standard non‑contrast studies to contrast‑enhanced angiography and sequential non‑contrast/contrast protocols—and serves as a key imaging modality for diagnosing mediastinal pathology.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
agent2_snippets.content: The mediastinum is the central compartment of the thorax and a key anatomic subdivision within the chest, serving as the direct site for a variety of thoracic computed tomography (CT) examinations. Chest CT—including CT angiography of the thorax with contrast, non‑contrast diagnostic thoracic CT, and related protocols that add contrast—constitutes a primary imaging modality for evaluating mediastinal structures and is classified under the broader group of chest and respiratory‑tract imaging procedures. Chest CT functions as the principal finding method for multiple mediastinal abnormalities, such as a mass of the mid‑mediastinum, a mass of the posterior mediastinum, a mass of the superior mediastinum, a general mediastinal mass, a mediastinal mass containing calcium, and mediastinal effusion. The “isa” relationships indicate that CT angiography of thorax with contrast, CT of breast, and Computerized axial tomography of thorax with contrast are direct subtypes of a chest CT examination. The two thoracic CT procedures described as diagnostic without contrast material, and diagnostic without contrast material followed by contrast material(s) and further sections, have a narrower relationship to the general chest CT category, reflecting that they represent more detailed or sequential imaging protocols within thoracic CT. The entry “computed tomography of chest: miliary pulmonary infiltrate” is linked to chest CT by a “has_finding_method” relationship, indicating that this particular CT technique is employed to identify the radiologic pattern of miliary pulmonary infiltrates in the lungs. Additionally, CT of breast is connected to the Arabic term تصوير الثدي الشعاعي (radiographic breast imaging) through an “inverse_isa” relationship, suggesting that while CT of breast is a specialized chest CT modality, it serves as a complementary or alternative imaging approach to conventional radiographic breast examinations.
